section.title: Let Us Go to the House of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents of David, third in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Where Psalm 120 cried from a distance and Psalm 121 promised help on the road, this psalm arrives: feet finally standing within Jerusalem's gates.
section.thesis: The joy of arriving at the house of the LORD in a city built for unity and justice moves the pilgrim to pray earnestly for Jerusalem's peace, a joy and a prayer fulfilled in Christ and the heavenly Jerusalem to come.
section.frameNote: Three movements: joyful arrival at Jerusalem's gates, verses 1 to 2, praise for the city's unity and justice, verses 3 to 5, and the call to pray for her peace, verses 6 to 9.

units.label: I was glad when they said to me

verses.text: I was glad when they said to me, "Let us go to the house of the LORD."
Our feet are standing in your gates, O Jerusalem.
Jerusalem is built up as a city united together,
where the tribes go up, the tribes of the LORD, as a testimony for Israel, to give thanks to the name of the LORD.
For there the thrones of judgment stand, the thrones of the house of David.

units.label: Pray for the peace of Jerusalem

verses.text: Pray for the {{shalom|peace}} of Jerusalem: "May those who love you prosper.
May there be peace within your walls, and prosperity inside your fortresses."
For the sake of my brothers and friends, I will say, "Peace be within you."
For the sake of the house of the LORD our God, I will seek your prosperity.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, security, and prosperity together. Jerusalem's very name plays on this word, so praying for the city's shalom is praying that it live up to its own name.

basic.context: Attributed to David, this Song of Ascents captures the pilgrim's joy at finally arriving in Jerusalem, standing within its gates after the anxious distance of Psalm 120 and the promised help of Psalm 121, and moves from personal gladness to a prayer for the whole city's peace.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes a specific pilgrim's arrival at Jerusalem's gates for worship, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern rejoicing in corporate worship and praying earnestly for the peace of God's city and people.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist prays for Jerusalem's shalom, a peace that means wholeness, security, and prosperity together, not merely an absence of war, playing on the city's own name which contains the same root.
basic.typology: The thrones of the house of David set for judgment anticipate the greater Son of David who judges in righteousness, and the joy of going up to the house of the LORD anticipates the joy of believers gathering as living stones in Christ's spiritual temple.
basic.christ: Jesus wept over Jerusalem's failure to know the things that make for peace, Luke 19:41-44, the very peace this psalm prays for, and He is Himself the greater Son of David whose throne of judgment will never fail.
basic.god: God dwells among His gathered people in the city He chose, receiving their thanks and establishing just rule there through the house of David, and He hears the prayers offered for that city's peace.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 19:41-44 shows Jesus weeping over Jerusalem's unrecognized peace. Hebrews 12:22 pictures believers as having come to the heavenly Jerusalem. Revelation 21:2 shows the new Jerusalem descending as the final fulfillment of this psalm's joy and peace.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's joy at arriving in Jerusalem to worship, and its prayer for the city's peace, are fulfilled in Christ and the heavenly Jerusalem, the true and lasting city of peace He is building for His people.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents attributed to David, moving from personal joy at arrival, verses 1 to 2, to communal praise of the city's unity and justice, verses 3 to 5, to a corporate prayer for peace, verses 6 to 9.
context.historical: Likely reflects the period after David brought the ark to Jerusalem and established it as the center of worship and of the throne of judgment, before or shortly after Solomon's temple was built.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 121's promised protection with the actual arrival at the destination, and anticipates the New Testament's vision of the heavenly Jerusalem as the final home of God's gathered people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express the joy of corporate worship at Jerusalem and to call God's people to pray earnestly for the peace and prosperity of the city where He has set His name and His throne of justice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific pilgrim's arrival and prayer for historic Jerusalem. It prescribes as an abiding pattern rejoicing in gathered worship and praying for the peace of God's people and God's city, ultimately the church and the new Jerusalem.

debates.question: Does 'the peace of Jerusalem' in verse 6 point only to the earthly city or beyond it?

views.label: Primarily the historic, earthly city of Jerusalem
views.case: The psalm's concrete details, gates, walls, fortresses, and the thrones of David's house, describe a real, physical city under real threat and in need of real peace.

views.label: A pattern fulfilled ultimately in the heavenly Jerusalem
views.case: Hebrews 12:22 and Revelation 21 take up Jerusalem as the pattern for God's final dwelling with His people, suggesting the psalm's prayer finds its complete answer beyond the earthly city.

debates.bearing: Christians rightly pray for the peace of the earthly city while recognizing that the psalm's deepest longing is answered in the peace Christ brings and the heavenly Jerusalem still to come.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist prays for Jerusalem's shalom, wholeness, security, and prosperity together, a word that echoes the city's own name. The tribes go up to give thanks, and thrones for judgment stand there, rooting justice in the house of David.

typology.type: The thrones of the house of David set for judgment, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the greater Son of David, whose throne of righteous judgment will stand forever, Isaiah 9:7, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: The joy of going up to the house of the LORD, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: The joy of believers gathered as living stones in Christ's spiritual house, 1 Peter 2:5, and their approach to the heavenly Jerusalem, Hebrews 12:22.

study.christ: Jesus wept over Jerusalem because it did not recognize the things that make for peace, Luke 19:41-44, the very shalom this psalm prays for. He is the greater Son of David whose throne of judgment, unlike the fallen throne in Jerusalem, will never be shaken, and He gathers His people into the true house of the LORD, His body and His church, and finally the heavenly Jerusalem, Hebrews 12:22, Revelation 21:2.
study.god: God dwells among His gathered people in the city He chose, receives their thanksgiving, establishes just rule there through David's house, and hears the prayers His people offer for that city's peace.

crossReferences.note: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's failure to know the things that make for peace, the very peace this psalm prays for.
Believers have come to Mount Zion and the heavenly Jerusalem, the fulfillment of this psalm's pilgrim joy.
The new Jerusalem descends as the final, lasting city of peace this psalm's prayer anticipates.

study.oneStory: This psalm's joy at arriving to worship in Jerusalem, and its prayer for the city's peace, are fulfilled in Christ, the greater Son of David, and in the heavenly Jerusalem He is building as the true and lasting home of His gathered people.

sermon.title: A Pilgrim's Journey
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 120 to 122 together as one pilgrim's journey, showing how the ascent moves from distress, through promised help, to the joy of arrival at Jerusalem's gates and the call to pray for the city's peace.

quiz.q: What made the psalmist glad, according to verse 1?

options.text: Others inviting him, 'Let us go to the house of the LORD.'
options.note: This is the exact occasion for his gladness, stated at the outset.
The gladness is tied to corporate worship, not material gain.
The psalm's joy is about worship and arrival, not conquest.
Harvest joy appears in other psalms, such as 126, not the stated cause here.

quiz.q: What is said to make Jerusalem strong, according to verse 3?

options.text: It is built up as a city united together.
options.note: This unity, not walls alone, is what the psalm highlights.

options.text: Its vast size compared to other cities.
options.note: The psalm emphasizes unity, not sheer size.
Trade is not mentioned; the emphasis is on unity and worship.
The text speaks of internal unity, not geographic isolation.

quiz.q: What stands in Jerusalem according to verse 5?

options.text: Thrones of judgment, the thrones of the house of David.
options.note: This roots the city's justice in the Davidic line.
The verse names thrones of righteous judgment tied to David, not idolatry.
The psalm does not mention commerce here.
Walls are mentioned later, in verse 7, but verse 5 specifically names thrones of judgment.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist urge people to do for Jerusalem in verse 6?

options.note: This direct exhortation opens the psalm's closing section.
The psalm calls for engaged prayer, not avoidance.
The concern here is prayer for peace, not a building project.

options.text: Pay increased taxes to the king.
options.note: Taxation is not the subject; prayer for peace is.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's vision of Jerusalem connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the heavenly Jerusalem and Christ, the greater Son of David whose throne endures forever.
options.note: Hebrews 12:22 and Luke 1:32-33 show this fulfillment.

options.text: It has no connection beyond ancient Israelite history.
options.note: The New Testament directly takes up both the Davidic throne and the Jerusalem imagery.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Jerusalem's fall in AD 70.
options.note: The psalm's focus is on typology and hope, not a specific date.

options.text: It shows Jesus rejecting Jerusalem entirely.
options.note: Jesus weeps over the city out of love, longing for its peace, not rejection of it.

questions: The psalmist was glad simply at the invitation to worship together. What is your own posture toward gathering with God's people, eager or reluctant?
Jerusalem's strength is described as unity, not merely walls. Where is disunity weakening the church or community you belong to?
The psalm ties Jerusalem's justice to David's throne. How does trusting Christ, the greater Son of David, shape how you respond to injustice you witness or experience?
The psalmist prays for Jerusalem's peace for the sake of brothers, friends, and the house of the LORD. Who and what are you actively praying for the peace and good of right now?

This psalm's joy and prayer find their fullest answer in the heavenly Jerusalem still to come. How does that future hope shape your patience with the imperfect gatherings and cities you belong to today?

translationNotes.note: The invitation 'Let us go to the house of the LORD' reflects the communal nature of pilgrimage, worship undertaken together rather than alone.
Built up as a city united together can also be rendered compact or tightly joined, an image drawn from Jerusalem's dense architecture as well as its social and covenantal unity.
Pray for the peace of Jerusalem contains a wordplay in Hebrew between Jerusalem and shalom, peace, so the prayer is in effect that the city live up to its own name.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 122 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 120-132). Verse count (9) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 122:1-9. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'A Pilgrim's Journey' (covering Psalms 120-122) at truthforlife.org.
